docs.title: About me
docs.text: NK

docs.title: Hi, I'm Nikos Kitsikoudis.
docs.text:
I'm an FPV racer and freestyle pilot, a 3D-printing enthusiast, and an electronics hobbyist and professional. FPV also got me into SMD repair, which has allowed me to bring several broken drone components back to life instead of replacing them.

GitHub

docs.title: Race quad
docs.text:
• Frame: Drippuh by SanderPuh
  • Motors: T-Hobby Velox V3 2050KV
  • Stack: HDZero Halo Stack
  • Antenna: Rush Cherry Y-Type

View the complete build →

docs.title: My FPV journey
docs.text:
I started flying FPV in September 2023 and entered racing in early 2024. Since then, racing has become a major part of the hobby for me. I have competed at the major German Aircrasher events as well as the MultiGP European Championship.

In 2026, I am taking the next step and attending more international events, with the goal of racing at as many major European competitions as possible.

docs.title: Race rankings & results
docs.text:
As of 14 July 2026, I am ranked #97 out of 622 pilots in the 2026 MultiGP Global Qualifier with a best three-lap time of 34.816 seconds.

3rd / 15

TSV Feldkirchen Central Europe RQ

13th / 40

Aircrasher Aichtal Central Europe RQ

15th / 39

Open Belgian Championship Heat 1

9th / 40

Aircrasher Aichtal DCS 2

My complete archive separates qualifying and final positions and links every result to its timing or event source.

View all race results → Meet Holy*s*quad →

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
My 2026 international race plans include:

  • FAI Belgium
  • FAI Italy
  • Potentially FAI Slovakia
  • MultiGP European Championship in Aichtal

At the MultiGP European Championship in Aichtal, I will also be part of the organizing team and helper crew.

docs.title: Milestones
docs.text:
• September 2023 – Started flying FPV
  • Early 2024 – Started FPV racing
  • 2024–2025 – Competed at the major German Aircrasher events
  • Before 2026 – Competed at the MultiGP European Championship
  • 2026 – Expanded into more international racing across Europe

docs.title: My Squad - Holy*s*quad
docs.text:
FPV racing is more fun with the people you share the pits, practice sessions and race weekends with. This page tracks the competitive results of our squad using the same field-normalized statistics as my personal race archive.

KC KidCe

Nikos Kitsikoudis

MD MaxDax

Maximilian Degen

BJ bajo

Jonas Bargenda

ZL ZeLaus

Claus Bargenda

docs.title: Performance comparison
docs.text:
The bars show each pilot's median final score for the regular competitive events in this archive. A score of 100 means a win, 50 is midfield and 0 is last place. Normalizing the position by field size makes different races comparable.

KidCe 202439% 202557% 202663%

Steady improvement into roughly the top third of the field.

MaxDax 202435% 202569% 202675%

A clear rise from the lower half to a typical top-quarter result.

bajo 202414% 202571% 202688%

The strongest jump in the squad, now regularly near the front.

ZeLaus 202417% 202512% 202618%

A 2026 recovery after the 2025 dip, with room to convert it into top-half finishes.

Pilot 2024 median Q / Final 2025 median Q / Final 2026 YTD median Q / Final 2026 top-half finals KidCe 44% / 39% 59% / 57% 62% / 63% 7 / 9 · 78% MaxDax 60% / 35% 68% / 69% 68% / 75% 9 / 9 · 100% bajo 21% / 14% 82% / 71% 92% / 88% 6 / 6 · 100% ZeLaus 22% / 17% 17% / 12% 21% / 18% 0 / 9 · 0%

These are descriptive trends, not a formal skill rating. Race formats and field strength still influence the numbers. A DCS multi-final weekend counts once in the final median through its official overall result; the child rounds are not counted again. Qualifying medians use the round-level qualifying tables because the combined PDFs do not publish one overall qualifying rank. Training, tests, fun races, unfinished events and season standings are excluded. A timing-page entry is not counted until a completed classification has been published.

docs.title: Event-by-event trends
docs.text: Each chart uses completed events with an exact published final ranking. The dashed line is a trailing five-race moving average. Hover or tap a point for the event, date and original placement. DCS multi-final weekends count once through their official overall result.

docs.title: Squad trend comparison
docs.text:
This view puts all four trailing five-race averages on one real calendar timeline. Each line begins after that pilot's fifth completed event, so short spikes matter less and the longer-term direction is easier to compare.

Comparison of the trailing five-race performance averages for KidCe, MaxDax, bajo and ZeLaus from 2024 through 2026.

docs.title: KidCe
docs.text:
Momentum to build on

The typical result has moved from the lower half in 2024 to roughly the top third in 2026, while the calendar now includes stronger international fields. The consistency work is showing—keep stacking clean race laps and the next step is turning more of those solid weekends into deep finals runs.

KidCe event-by-event final performance and trailing five-race moving average from 2024 through 2026.

docs.title: MaxDax
docs.text:
A strong upward trajectory

A 35% median in 2024 has become 75% in 2026, with every recorded 2026 final landing in the top half. That is real, repeatable progress. Keep refining race-day consistency—the pace is already there to challenge further up the order.

MaxDax event-by-event final performance and trailing five-race moving average from 2024 through 2026.

docs.title: bajo
docs.text:
Proof that the training works

The rise from a 14% median in 2024 to 88% in 2026 is the squad's biggest jump, and the recent results are regularly near the front. Keep protecting that speed with clean, controlled runs; the data says the work is converting into results.

bajo event-by-event final performance and trailing five-race moving average from 2024 through 2026.

docs.title: ZeLaus
docs.text:
Keep building the recovery

The 2026 median has climbed back from 12% to 18% while the schedule includes larger international fields and valuable high-pressure race experience. Every completed event adds useful laps. Keep training for clean, repeatable runs—the recovery is a base to build the next step on.

ZeLaus event-by-event final performance and trailing five-race moving average from 2024 through 2026.

docs.title: Verified race results
docs.text:
The archive below contains regular competitive events from 2024 onward. Open a pilot to see the complete qualifying and final tables. A dash means the event did not publish a separate final classification. Bold DCS rows are the official overall event result. Their indented rows show the multi-final rounds that were combined for that result. Where an event uses a dropped round, the official PDF remains authoritative for that rule and its tie-breakers.

For my own detailed results, see the separate KidCe race archive.

MaxDax · Maximilian Degen · verified archive ZeLaus · Claus Bargenda · verified archive bajo · Jonas Bargenda · verified archive

  1. The published event-wide LiveFPV result is used here. A separate Main B page reports a bracket-only position, which is not an overall championship rank. ↩

  2. The 2026 championship is still running. Friedrichshafen is scheduled as the final scoring event, so these are interim positions checked on 14 July 2026. ↩↩

  3. FPVScores publishes the 53-pilot qualifying order and the elimination heats, but its aggregate “Race Time” list is not a bracket result. The final positions were reconstructed from those heats using the official FAI Scenario B classification rule: pilots eliminated at the same stage are ordered by their provisional qualifying rank; pilots outside the top 32 retain their qualifying order. ↩↩↩

  4. These results come from the organiser's provisional classification sheet photographed at the venue. The official event page confirms the date, venue and Scenario B format; a public final result document was not available when this archive was updated. ↩↩↩

docs.title: Race results
docs.text:
This is my verified race archive under the pilot handle KidCe. The results were checked against MultiGP, FPVScores, Aircrasher LiveFPV, BMR LiveFPV and Copterrace. Training sessions, test events, unfinished events and placeholders are not included. A timing-page entry only counts once a completed classification has been published. Last checked: 14 July 2026.

View my MultiGP profile Meet Holy*s*quad

docs.title: Performance over time
docs.text:
The trend is clearly upward rather than flat midfield performance. My median final score improved from 39% in 2024 to 57% in 2025 and 63% in 2026 so far. In practical terms, my typical result moved from the lower half of the field to roughly the top third.

Qualifying medians: 44% in 2024, 59% in 2025 and 62% in 2026. Final medians: 39% in 2024, 57% in 2025 and 63% in 2026.

docs.title: Event-by-event performance
docs.text:
This chart shows every completed event with an exact published final ranking. Hover or tap a point to see the event, date and original placement. The dashed line is a trailing five-race moving average, which reduces event-to-event noise. Official DCS weekend results are counted once; their individual multi-final rounds, unfinished events and result ranges are omitted.

Event-by-event final performance and its trailing five-race moving average trend upward overall between 2024 and 2026.

In 2026, 7 of 9 event results (78%) ended in the top half, compared with 1 of 3 (33%) in 2024. The median final score rises from 39% → 57% → 63% across the three seasons.

Individual results still vary with race format and field strength, so this is not a straight line or a formal skill rating. The season-level direction is positive.

The score is normalized for field size: (field size - position) / (field size - 1) × 100. A winner scores 100, exact midfield is around 50 and last place scores 0. Each standalone competition counts once. For a DCS multi-final weekend, only the official combined event result counts toward the final median; its round results remain visible in the table but are not counted again. Qualifying medians use the published round-level tables because the combined reports do not provide one overall qualifying rank. Fun races, unfinished events, season standings and finals without an exact position are excluded. n is the number of results included.

How to read the tables

Each position is linked to its source. A dash means that the event format or published result did not include a separate leaderboard for that stage. When a timing platform and a consolidated event result differ, the consolidated result is used. Bold DCS rows are the official result for the complete event. The indented rows below them are the individual multi-final rounds that feed into that result. Where the format uses a dropped round, the official PDF already applies that rule and any tie-breakers. Raw timing-system views can count DNS or unclassified pilots differently.

docs.title: Club and fun races
docs.text:
These events are part of my racing history, but are kept separate from the main competitive archive.

Date Event Qualifying Final 19 Jun 2026 BMR 10 Year Anniversary FunRace 4 / 11 4 / 11 13 Jun 2026 PreRace FunyFace 4 / 11 2 / 11 15 Feb 2026 Bootcamp Fun Race · Day 2 2 / 9 2 / 9 14 Feb 2026 Bootcamp Fun Race · Day 1 2 / 7 2 / 7 28 Sep 2025 Oberschleißheim Final Crashout — 5 / 9 1 Aug 2025 Korntal pre-GQ Funrace 10 / 15 10 / 15 18 Jul 2025 BMR FunFly SupaGuy 6 / 24 5 / 24 2 May 2025 BMR Fun Race 9 / 25 6 / 25 1 May 2025 After-Show-Party 8 / 19 6 / 19

  1. Live standings checked on 14 July 2026. The ranking will move as more pilots submit results. ↩↩

  2. The 2026 DCS season is still running. Friedrichshafen is scheduled as the final scoring event, so this is an interim position rather than a year-end result. ↩

  3. The official Aircrasher PDF is used for the BMR weekend result. The component platforms classify stages and inactive pilots differently: BMR LiveFPV shows 8th rather than 7th in the Regional Qualifier and 33rd rather than 32nd in DCS 2 qualifying. The table retains the consolidated MultiGP values for those two stage details. ↩

  4. MultiGP publishes 46th in its official Race Results and 68th in its Qualifying Ranking. LiveFPV exposes different stage views (48th in its qualifying session and 10th in the Main D bracket), so those stage positions are not mixed into the overall result shown here. ↩

  5. Copterrace does not publish an exact overall final rank for this double-elimination bracket. The verified elimination path places KidCe between 9th and 12th, so the archive shows the supported range instead of treating the unrelated race-time statistic as a final position. ↩

  6. FPVScores publishes the 53-pilot qualifying order and the elimination heats, but its aggregate “Race Time” list is not a bracket result. The final position was reconstructed from those heats using the official FAI Scenario B classification rule: pilots eliminated at the same stage are ordered by their provisional qualifying rank. ↩

  7. The result comes from the organiser's provisional classification sheet photographed at the venue. The official event page confirms the date, venue and Scenario B format; a public final result document was not available when this archive was updated. ↩

docs.title: Articles
docs.text: Race reports, builds and personal experiences from my life in FPV.

docs.title: A new home for my FPV notes
A new home for my FPV notes
docs.text: The small FPV wiki is becoming a personal website. This is where I will share longer stories, race reports and build logs, while technical notes keep their dedicated place in the wiki.

docs.title: What you will find here
docs.text:
Some lessons fit into a short guide. Others need a story: What was the idea behind a build? What went wrong at a race? What would I do differently next time? That is what these articles are for.

This is an example post

Edit it, copy it as a template or delete it once your first article is ready.

docs.title: My quad builds
docs.text:
This is where I document the quads I build and fly: complete component lists, design decisions, setup notes and what I would change next time.

Adding a new build

Copy one of the existing pages, replace the component list and add it to the Builds navigation in mkdocs.yml.

docs.title: 2026 Racing Setup
docs.text: My main race quad is built for a direct, consistent feel and the low-latency HDZero video system.

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Component Part Frame Drippuh by SanderPuh Motors T-Hobby Velox V3 2050KV / T-Hobby F50 under testing Flight controller HDZero Halo FC, ICM42866P gyro ESC HDZero Halo ESC, AM32 Video system HDZero Antenna Rush Cherry Y-Type Receiver Add receiver Propellers Add propellers Battery Add battery Dry weight 262–285 g, depending on motors and front brace

docs.title: Motor testing
docs.text: I am currently testing the T-Hobby F50 motors and considering switching the entire racing setup to them. Compared with the Velox V3, the F50 configuration saves 17 g with the same frame configuration.

docs.title: Weight comparison
docs.text: Motors Frame configuration Dry weight T-Hobby Velox V3 2050KV One front brace 285 g T-Hobby Velox V3 2050KV No front brace 279 g T-Hobby F50 One front brace 268 g T-Hobby F50 No front brace 262 g

docs.title: Budget Build
docs.text: An affordable 5-inch FPV build focused on solid components, durability and a good flight feel. Choose the video system that best fits your budget and existing equipment.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
Component Part Price Frame Drippuh €38 Flight controller JHEMCU F722 NOXE €35 ESC SEQURE Blueson A1 70A 8S AM32 — Motors T-Hobby Velox V3 2050KV, set of four approx. €45 on a good AliExpress sale Propellers Gemfan 5128.0 Fury — Receiver Happymodel EP1 clone €10 Video system Your choice —

Known subtotal: €128, excluding the ESC, propellers and video system.

docs.title: JHEMCU F722 NOXE
docs.text: I flew the JHEMCU F722 NOXE for around one and a half years and have been extremely happy with it. Mine has proven very durable and has always run cleanly and reliably.

docs.title: SEQURE Blueson A1 70A
docs.text:
I previously had bad experiences with a 65A AM32 ESC, although I was flying it without a capacitor at the time. I later tried the 70A Blueson A1. It uses the proven Toshiba TPHR8504PL MOSFETs, which were also used on my all-time favorite ESC, the Foxeer 60A Slim.

I experienced one hiccup with the Blueson as well, but I am fairly confident that the issue has been resolved since fitting a good capacitor. See ESC capacitors: why they matter for selection and installation guidance.

docs.title: Build notes
docs.text:
• Use a good-quality, correctly rated capacitor with the ESC.
  • The Gemfan 5128.0 Fury propellers offer great durability and a great flight feel.
  • Look out for AliExpress sales to get a set of four T-Hobby Velox V3 motors for around €45.
  • Select an analog, HDZero, Walksnail or DJI video system depending on your priorities and budget.

Prices

Prices are approximate and may change depending on the seller, sale and shipping costs.

docs.title: 3.5" 1S Endurance Drone
docs.text: An experimental 3.5-inch drone optimized for long flight times on a single-cell battery. Its lightweight frame is 3D-printed from PPA-CF.

docs.title: Project goals
docs.text:
• Maximize endurance with a 1S power system
  • Develop a lightweight, printable PPA-CF frame
  • Find an efficient combination of motors, propellers and electronics
  • Document flight time, weight and durability

Project page in progress

The component list, frame files, print settings and test results will be added during development.

A wiki that grows with the hobby

Create a new page under docs/wiki/, then add it to nav in mkdocs.yml.

docs.title: Fundamentals
docs.text:
Concepts, settings and terminology explained clearly.

Understanding rates →

docs.title: Repairs
docs.text:
Symptoms, diagnostic steps and repair notes from the workbench.

Happymodel 2G4 →

docs.title: ESC capacitors
docs.text: An ESC capacitor helps absorb voltage spikes and electrical noise generated by the battery leads, ESC and motors. This reduces stress on the electronics and can improve the reliability of the power system and video feed.

docs.title: What to use
docs.text:
• Choose a good-quality low-ESR electrolytic capacitor.
  • Its voltage rating must be higher than the fully charged battery voltage, with sensible safety margin.
  • Use a capacitance appropriate for the build and ESC manufacturer's recommendation.
  • Never exceed the capacitor's temperature and voltage ratings.

For a typical 6S 5-inch build, a quality 35V low-ESR capacitor is a common starting point. Larger or particularly noisy setups may benefit from more capacitance, provided the installation remains safe and practical.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Connect the capacitor across the ESC battery input:

  • Positive capacitor leg to battery positive
  • Negative capacitor leg, marked by the stripe on an electrolytic capacitor, to battery negative
  • Keep the leads as short as practical
  • Insulate exposed conductors and secure the capacitor against vibration and crashes

Polarity matters

Electrolytic capacitors are polarized. Connecting one backwards or exceeding its voltage rating can destroy it and may cause it to vent or burst.

docs.title: Capacitors are not a cure-all
docs.text: A capacitor can reduce spikes and noise, but it cannot compensate for damaged components, poor solder joints, undersized wiring or an overloaded ESC. If failures or resets continue, inspect the entire power system rather than relying on a larger capacitor alone.

docs.title: Understanding rates
docs.text: Rates determine how strongly and how quickly your quad responds to stick movements. This page is prepared as a starting point for your own notes.

docs.title: The three important values
docs.text:
Value Effect Center Sensitivity Response around the center of the stick Max Rate Maximum rotation speed at full stick deflection Expo Shapes the transition between center and full deflection

Do not copy blindly

Rates are highly personal. Make gradual changes and test them in a safe environment.

docs.text: This is where you can document your current values, screenshots and the reasoning behind your setup.
